1: The twelve sons of Israel (Jacob) open the Chronicler’s detailed genealogies. Paul’s statement that ‘the Messiah, according to the flesh, is from Israel’ (Romans 9:5) presupposes this covenant-people framework: the Messiah does not emerge from humanity in the abstract but from the specific covenant family of Jacob. The Chronicler traces from Adam (1 Chronicles 1:1) to all Israel (1 Chronicles 9:1) before narrowing to the Davidic line in 1 Chronicles 2 — a movement from universal humanity to particular covenant community to specific Messianic lineage that mirrors the NT’s own Christological logic.
2: The twelve sons of Israel complete (Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, Asher). The tribal completeness of Israel matters for Messianic expectation: Revelation 7:4-8 seals 12,000 from each tribe, and the new Jerusalem has twelve gates named for the twelve tribes (Revelation 21:12). Christ comes as the fulfillment of the covenant with all twelve tribes, not merely Judah’s branch alone — the universal scope of his redemption recapitulates the full tribal structure.
3: Judah’s sons by his Canaanite wife Bathshua (Er, Onan, Shelah) and by Tamar (Perez, Zerah). Hebrews 7:14 grounds the high-priestly legitimacy question in Christ’s Judahite descent: ‘It is evident that our Lord was descended from Judah.’ The mention of Judah’s sons here is the first node of the Messianic genealogy narrowing to the tribe prophesied in Genesis 49:10 (‘the scepter shall not depart from Judah’). Three sons of Judah (Er, Onan) died under divine judgment before Perez; the Messianic line only survived through an unexpected avenue.
4: Tamar bore Perez and Zerah to Judah. Matthew names Tamar first among the four women in his genealogy (Matthew 1:3), drawing attention to the irregular path by which the covenant line descended: Tamar used bold legal strategy (invoking levirate rights) to continue the line when Judah’s family would have suppressed it. The twin-birth narrative (Genesis 38:27-30) — Zerah put out his hand first but Perez ‘broke through’ (pāraṣ) — gives Perez his name (‘breaking through’). The Messianic line runs through the one who unexpectedly broke through: a pattern that recurs in David (youngest son), the virgin birth, and the resurrection.
5: Perez fathered Hezron and Hamul. The Ruth genealogy begins at this point (Ruth 4:18: ‘These are the generations of Perez’), and Matthew 1:3 continues the chain from Perez through Hezron. Hezron is the Israelite tribal ancestor whose family became numerous in Egypt (Genesis 46:12; Numbers 26:21); the Messianic line thus passes through the period of Egyptian bondage, emerging in the exodus-generation community.
6: The sons of Zerah: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara — known for wisdom (1 Kings 4:31 lists Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Darda as the wise men Solomon surpassed). The wisdom tradition within Judah’s Zerahite branch stands as a foil to the Messiah who is ‘the wisdom of God’ (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30) and ‘in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge’ (Colossians 2:3). The Zerahites represent created wisdom; Christ is the source and substance of all wisdom.
7: Achar (Achan) the troubler of Israel — his violation of the sacred ban at Jericho (Josh 7) brought covenant death on the community. The name-pun (‘Achar’ = ‘troubler’) is the Chronicler’s editorial judgment. Achar’s act is the inverse of Christ’s: Achar took what was under the ban, bringing death on the innocent (Achan’s family executed with him); Christ became a curse for us (Galatians 3:13), taking the ban-death on himself so that the community might live. The Zerahite line’s most notorious member is defined by covenant-curse; the Perezite line’s terminus is defined by covenant-redemption.
8: Azariah the son of Ethan — genealogical continuity in the Zerahite line. The preservation of this lineage within Judah attests that even the non-Messianic branches of the tribe were maintained within the covenant community. The Chronicler’s comprehensive genealogy reflects the conviction that the whole people of Israel belongs to YHWH’s purposes, not only the Davidic line.
9: Hezron’s three sons — Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai/Caleb. The branching here is critical: Ram is the Messianic branch (v10: Ram fathered Amminadab → David line); Caleb leads to the Judahite clans including Bethlehem (v51: ‘Salma, the father of Bethlehem’); Jerahmeel leads to the southern Judean clans. The Messianic birthplace (Bethlehem) and the Messianic ancestor (Ram) both trace to Hezron, making this genealogical fork a doubly significant Messianic node.
10: Ram fathered Amminadab — Matthew 1:4 exactly. Amminadab is the father of Nahshon (v11), who served as the prince/leader of the tribe of Judah in the wilderness (Numbers 2:3), the tribe that marched first in Israel’s formation (Numbers 10:14). The Messianic line ran through Israel’s leading tribe’s leading family in the wilderness generation. Amminadab’s daughter Elisheba married Aaron (Exodus 6:23), connecting the priestly and Judahite royal lines in this generation — a structural prefiguration of Christ who holds both offices (Hebrews 7:14: priest according to Melchizedek, from the tribe of Judah).
11: Nahshon fathered Salma/Salmon — Matthew 1:4. Nahshon was the Judahite wilderness leader; his son Salmon is identified in Matthew 1:5 as the husband of Rahab (the Canaanite innkeeper of Jericho whose scarlet thread marked her house, Josh 2). The insertion of Rahab into the Messianic line at this point is striking: a Gentile, a former prostitute, and a Canaanite enters the covenant genealogy through faith (‘by faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient,’ Hebrews 11:31). Matthew names her explicitly (Matthew 1:5) as one of the four women in his genealogy, each of whom represents a boundary-crossing in the Messianic line’s descent.
12: Salmon/Salma fathered Boaz, and Boaz fathered Obed — Matthew 1:5. Boaz married Ruth the Moabitess (Ruth 4:13-22); the Book of Ruth narrates the covenant-faithful circumstances of this union. Ruth is the second Gentile woman named in Matthew’s genealogy (Matthew 1:5). The Messianic line passed through two successive Gentile women — Rahab and Ruth — as if to signal from the pre-Davidic genealogical record that the coming King would be not Israel’s king only but the world’s.
13: Obed fathered Jesse; Jesse fathered Eliab his firstborn. Jesse lived in Bethlehem (1 Samuel 17:12) and is the fulfillment of the Ephrathah geographic link (v19, 50-51: Caleb’s Ephrathah = Bethlehem). Isaiah 11:1 prophesies ‘a shoot from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots shall bear fruit’ — the Messianic title ‘Root of Jesse’ (Isaiah 11:10) is cited in Romans 15:12 as fulfilled in Christ: ‘the root of Jesse will come, even he who arises to rule the Gentiles; in him will the Gentiles hope.’ Revelation 5:5 further applies it: ‘the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered.’
14: Abinadab (Jesse’s second son) and Shimea his third. The older brothers are passed over; 1 Sam 16 records Samuel’s expectation that the firstborn Eliab was YHWH’s anointed, but YHWH’s criterion is the heart, not appearance. The pattern of the unexpected son (Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Joseph the favored, David the youngest) reaches its NT culmination in Jesus — born not in a palace but in a stable, from an obscure Galilean family, rejected by the religious establishment (John 1:11).
15: Nethanel (4th), Raddai (5th), Ozem (6th), and David the seventh. David is the seventh — the complete number — and the youngest, least expected son. His anointing (1 Samuel 16:1-13: ‘The LORD does not look on outward appearance... but on the heart’) is the paradigm for divine election: the chosen one is consistently the one who does not qualify by human standards. Matthew 1:6 names David as ‘the king,’ the only king in the genealogy to receive this title — the pivot of the whole Matthean list around which the genealogy is structured in two arcs (pre-David and post-David).
16: Jesse’s daughters: Zeruiah (mother of Joab, Abishai, and Asahel — David’s military commanders) and Abigail (mother of Amasa). The inclusion of daughters in the genealogy is notable; the Chronicler records the female relatives of David who shaped his court and military. The female line within the Davidic family is significant for the NT genealogical argument about Mary’s Davidic descent (Luke 1:32 says the angel tells Mary that Jesus ‘will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High, and the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David’).
17: Abigail bore Amasa, whose father was Jether the Ishmaelite. A Messianic-family member fathered by an Ishmaelite — one more example of the non-Israelite threads woven into Israel’s royal genealogy. The covenant line preserved through Judah includes not only Canaanite (Tamar), Canaanite-Jericho (Rahab), Moabite (Ruth), and Ishmaelite (Amasa’s father) ancestry, demonstrating by genealogical record that the Messiah’s family tree was already international before Christ’s universal mission made it explicit.
18: Caleb (son of Hezron, v9) fathered Azubah and Jerioth by his wife. The Calebite genealogy begins a long section (vv18-55) tracing the clans of western Judah. Caleb’s line is historically associated with Bethlehem (v51: ‘Salma, the father of Bethlehem’), the Messianic birthplace (Micah 5:2). The non-Messianic Judahite clans in vv18-55 are part of the tribal inheritance of Judah within which the Messianic promise operated.
19: When Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath (Ephrathah), who bore him Hur. Ephrathah/Ephrath is the name of the Bethlehem district — Bethlehem Ephrathah (Micah 5:2; Ruth 4:11; Genesis 35:19). Caleb’s marriage to Ephrathah weaves the Messianic birthplace name into the genealogical record: Bethlehem Ephrathah is not merely a geographic location but a covenant-lineage identity embedded in Judah’s tribal history. Matthew 2:6 cites Micah 5:2 as predicting Bethlehem as the Messiah’s birthplace, and Luke 2:4 notes that Joseph traveled to Bethlehem ‘because he was of the house and lineage of David.’
20: Hur fathered Uri; Uri fathered Bezalel. Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur was the master craftsman filled with the Spirit of God to construct the tabernacle (Exodus 31:2-5; 1 Chronicles 35:30-35). His Spirit-filled craftsmanship in building YHWH’s dwelling place is a typological anticipation of the One through whom the true tabernacle was made — the Word became flesh and ‘dwelt (eskēnōsen, pitched his tent) among us’ (John 1:14). Bezalel built the physical dwelling; Christ is the substance of what that dwelling signified.
21: Hezron (at age 60) fathered Segub by the daughter of Machir/Manasseh, and Segub fathered Jair. The intermarriage with Machir/Manasseh reflects the territorial complexity of the Transjordanian tribes. The preservation of Jair’s settlement history (23 cities in Gilead) shows how genealogy and territorial inheritance were intertwined — the Messianic promise of inheriting the land (Genesis 12:7; 1 Chronicles 15:18) is embedded in the tribal land-allocation history that genealogies like this record.
22: Jair held twenty-three cities in Gilead, including Kenath and its sixty towns, taken from Geshur and Aram. The territorial expansion of Judah’s descendant into Transjordanian areas reflects the breadth of Israel’s covenant-inheritance. The eschatological inheritance promised to Christ — ‘Ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession’ (Psalms 2:8) — is the consummation of the land-inheritance pattern that these genealogical territorial notes trace from Judah outward.
23: Geshur and Aram took back Havvoth-jair and Kenath with their villages. The ebb and flow of territorial control in the Transjordan — towns gained and lost across generations — underscores the conditional nature of the temporal land-possession. The Messianic inheritance (Hebrews 9:15: ‘the promised eternal inheritance’) is not subject to Aramean recapture; it is secured by the death of the mediator and cannot be lost.
24: After Hezron died in Caleb-Ephrathah, his wife Abijah bore Ashhur the father of Tekoa. Tekoa is the hometown of Amos (Amos 1:1), the shepherd-prophet whose message of covenant justice anticipated the prophetic tradition culminating in John the Baptist and Christ. The Messianic genealogy is embedded in a landscape of towns — Bethlehem, Tekoa, Hebron — that become prophetic settings in Israel’s history.
25: Sons of Jerahmeel (the firstborn of Hezron): Ram (his firstborn), Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah. The Jerahmeelites occupied the Negev of Judah (1 Samuel 27:10) and were part of the southern Judean tribal network. The Messianic line did not run through Jerahmeel (it went through Ram son of Hezron, not this Ram son of Jerahmeel), but the Jerahmeelites were part of the covenant community of Judah in whom the promise resided.
26: Jerahmeel also had another wife, named Atarah; she was the mother of Onam. The polygamous household structure of early Judah is recorded without editorial comment; the Chronicler’s interest is genealogical completeness, not domestic ideal. The Messianic line descended through families whose domestic arrangements were often irregular — Tamar (levirate), Rahab (pagan convert), Ruth (foreign widow), Bathsheba (adultery) — establishing that covenant transmission does not depend on moral perfection in the recipients.
27: Sons of Ram (firstborn of Jerahmeel): Maaz, Jamin, and Eker. These Jerahmeelite clans occupied territories in the Negev. Brief genealogical notation; they are members of the tribe of Judah, the covenant-bearing tribe from which Messiah was promised (Genesis 49:10; Hebrews 7:14).
28: Sons of Onam: Shammai and Jada. Shammai’s sons: Nadab and Abishur. These Jerahmeelite names represent the extended Judahite clan structure whose land and identity were preserved through the exile. The Chronicler’s post-exilic community needed to know their genealogical standing within Judah’s tribal inheritance — covenant membership was demonstrated through family record.
29: Abishur’s wife Abihail bore him Ahban and Molid. The Jerahmeelite genealogy continues; brief entry noting that the full breadth of Judah’s clans is maintained in the covenant register, even those with no direct role in the Messianic line. The eschatological fulfillment includes all twelve tribes (Rev 7; Revelation 21:12), not only David’s branch.
30: Sons of Nadab: Seled (died childless) and Appaim. The notation ‘but Seled died without children’ is a genealogical dead-end within the Jerahmeelite line — a reminder that biological continuity was not guaranteed. The Messianic line’s survival through such genealogical hazards (Er and Onan died, Seled died childless) underscores the providential preservation of the covenant chain.
31: Appaim’s son: Ishi; Ishi’s son: Sheshan; Sheshan had a son Ahlai. The Jerahmeelite line continues; the covenant community of Judah is comprehensively recorded. Sheshan’s situation (no sons, only daughters, v34) leads to a notable genealogical solution in vv35-41.
32: Sons of Jada (Shammai’s brother): Jether and Jonathan; Jether died without children. Another dead-end in the Jerahmeelite line. The genealogical gaps and failures within Judah’s non-Messianic branches contrast with the unbroken chain of the Ram → David → Solomon → Zerubbabel → Joseph line, which the Chronicler preserves with special care in 1 Chronicles 3.
33: Jonathan’s sons: Peleth and Zaza. These are descendants of Ram son of Jerahmeel (different from Ram son of Hezron in the Messianic line). The Jerahmeelite line closes here; they ‘were the descendants of Jerahmeel’ (v33b). The Chronicler’s comprehensive recording of all Judahite branches reflects the post-exilic need to document covenant identity — who belonged to Judah’s tribal community and could claim land-inheritance.
34: Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. The absence of male heirs created a genealogical problem the Chronicler resolves in v35 through the unusual arrangement of giving his daughter to his Egyptian slave Jarha. This mirrors the broader pattern of the covenant line’s survival through boundary-crossing arrangements — the Messianic genealogy consistently includes unexpected human instruments.
35: Sheshan gave his daughter to his servant Jarha as wife; she bore him Attai. An Egyptian slave enters the genealogy of Judah through marriage — another non-Israelite woven into Judah’s covenant register. The pattern of Gentile inclusion in the Messianic people’s genealogy (Tamar the Canaanite, Rahab, Ruth the Moabitess, Jarha the Egyptian) is a genealogical foreshadowing of the NT’s inclusion of Gentiles in the covenant community (Ephesians 2:11-13; Galatians 3:28-29).
36: Attai fathered Nathan; Nathan fathered Zabad. The Jerahmeelite line continues through the Egyptian-descended Attai into subsequent generations. Brief entry; these are members of the Judahite covenant community whose names are preserved in YHWH’s genealogical record — a reminder that those whose names are written in covenant history point toward those whose names are written in the Lamb’s book of life (Revelation 21:27).
37: Zabad fathered Ephlal; Ephlal fathered Obed. The Jerahmeelite genealogical chain continues toward the time of the monarchy. These are the clans of Judah who formed the covenant community within which the Davidic house and Messianic line were embedded.
38: Obed fathered Jehu; Jehu fathered Azariah. The Jerahmeelite line, now twelve generations from Hezron, represents the breadth of the Judahite covenant community across the pre-monarchic and monarchic periods. The covenant community’s depth across generations is the context for Paul’s statement that ‘not all who are descended from Israel belong to Israel’ (Romans 9:6) — genealogical membership in the covenant people does not guarantee personal covenant faith.
39: Azariah fathered Helez; Helez fathered Eleasah. The later Jerahmeelite generations likely extended into the period of the divided monarchy. Brief entry; covenant community continuity across Judah’s tribal branches.
40: Eleasah fathered Sismai; Sismai fathered Shallum. The Jerahmeelite line concludes shortly after this; the Chronicler’s genealogical register extends into the period of the late monarchy or early exile. The collective genealogical record of Judah was preserved through the Babylonian exile and restored in the post-exilic community — the same community for which the Messianic hope was most urgent.
41: Shallum fathered Jekamiah; Jekamiah fathered Elishama. The Jerahmeelite genealogy closes. The cumulative Judahite genealogy in 1 Chronicles 2:3-55 establishes the tribal context of the Messianic promise: it is a tribal, geographic, and familial identity that is covenant-given, not merely ethnic. The Messiah emerges from a specific people, place, and family — and the breadth of the Judahite register shows the whole community that surrounded and supported the Messianic line.
42: Sons of Caleb (Chelubai son of Hezron): his firstborn Mesha (father of Ziph) and his son Mareshah (father of Hebron). Hebron is David’s first capital (2 Samuel 2:11); Caleb’s line is associated with the very city where David first reigned. The Messianic king’s first royal city was already embedded in his ancestor Caleb’s genealogical territory.
43: Sons of Hebron: Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema. These are the Hebronite clans of the Calebite branch of Judah — brief entry. The Hebron connection links the Calebite genealogy to the Davidic monarchy’s origins.
44: Shema fathered Raham (father of Jorkeam); Rekem fathered Shammai. Calebite sub-clans of Judah; brief genealogical continuity. These clans occupied the Judean hills and Shephelah through which Israel’s history moved toward the monarchy.
45: Shammai’s son: Maon; Maon was the father of Beth-zur. Beth-zur was a significant fortified site in Judah (Nehemiah 3:16; Joshua 15:58) — a fortress-town whose founding is traced to the Calebite genealogy. The Chronicler’s interest in civic founders reflects the post-exilic community’s need to establish territorial legitimacy through genealogical record.
46: Caleb’s concubine Ephah bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez; Haran fathered Gazez. The Calebite genealogy branches through concubine lines as well as wives — reflecting the complex household structures of pre-monarchic Judah. The covenant community included families whose structures were legally complex, prefiguring the NT’s expansion of covenant membership beyond strict ethnic or genealogical definitions.
47: Sons of Jahdai: Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph. These are clans within the broader Calebite orbit. Brief entry; covenant community of Judah in the pre-monarchic period.
48: Caleb’s concubine Maacah bore Sheber and Tirhanah. Additional Calebite lines through concubine Maacah. The inclusivity of the Chronicler’s genealogical record — tracing lines through concubines as well as wives — reflects the comprehensive preservation of all Judahite covenant identity, whatever the domestic circumstances.
49: Maacah bore Shaaph (father of Madmannah) and Sheva (father of Machbenah and Gibea); Caleb’s daughter was Achsah. Achsah is the daughter of Caleb who famously asked her father for the springs of water after receiving the Negev land as her inheritance portion (Joshua 15:16-19; Judges 1:12-15) — a woman who boldly claimed her covenant inheritance. The detail of Achsah’s inheritance-claiming echoes forward to the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27) and foreshadows the NT principle that in Christ there is ‘neither male nor female’ in terms of covenant inheritance (Galatians 3:28-29).
50: These were the descendants of Caleb. The sons of Hur (firstborn of Ephrathah/Caleb’s wife): Shobal (father of Kiriath-jearim), Salma (father of Bethlehem), and Hareph (father of Beth-gader). Salma is listed here as the ‘father of Bethlehem’ — the Messianic birthplace (Micah 5:2) is embedded in the Calebite genealogy as a civic foundation attributed to Salma son of Hur son of Caleb’s wife Ephrathah. The genealogy of the Messiah’s birthplace is woven into the genealogy of Judah’s clans.
51: Salma was the father of Bethlehem; Hareph was the father of Beth-gader. Bethlehem’s Calebite founding confirms that the Messianic birthplace is not merely Davidic (David was from Bethlehem by birth) but reaches back through Judah’s clan structure to Caleb. Micah 5:2 (‘Bethlehem Ephrathah, you who are too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel’) is cited in Matthew 2:6 as the scriptural warrant for the wise men’s destination. Luke 2:4 notes Joseph’s journey to Bethlehem ‘because he was of the house and lineage of David.’
52: Sons of Shobal (father of Kiriath-jearim): Haroeh and half of the Menuhoth. Kiriath-jearim is where the Ark of the Covenant resided for twenty years before David brought it to Jerusalem (1 Samuel 7:1-2; 1 Chronicles 13:5-6). The Ark’s resting place in Calebite Kiriath-jearim before its Davidic enthronement in Jerusalem is a type of the Messianic king awaiting his full installation — the Ark in the village before reaching its temple-home.
53: Clans of Kiriath-jearim: the Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, and Mishraites; from these came the Zorathites and Eshtaolites. Zorah and Eshtaol are the towns associated with Samson’s tribe of Dan (Judges 13:2; 1 Chronicles 16:31), but here Zorathites are listed as Calebite-Judahite clans — reflecting the geographic overlap at the tribal boundary. Brief entry.
54: Sons of Salma (father of Bethlehem): Bethlehem, the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, half the Manahathites, and the Zorites. Bethlehem appears again — Salma’s descendants are the clans inhabiting Bethlehem and its surrounding villages. The Netophathites (Netophah near Bethlehem) were among the returning exiles (Nehemiah 7:26). The repeated identification of Bethlehem with the Salmahite-Calebite genealogy establishes the Messianic city as a deeply rooted covenant-geography.
55: Clans of scribes at Jabez: Tirathites, Shimeathites, and Sucathites — these are Kenites descended from Hammath, the father of the house of Rechab. The inclusion of Kenite scribal guilds within Judah’s genealogical register reflects the absorption of non-Israelite groups into the covenant community through shared calling and function. The Rechabites are praised in Jer 35 as models of covenant faithfulness; their scribal descendants are embedded in Judah’s tribal record. The Chronicler’s comprehensive genealogy of Judah closes with this outward-facing note of inclusion, prefiguring the eschatological community in which people from all nations are enrolled in the covenant register (Isaiah 56:3-7; Revelation 21:24-27).
